meta.title: Original Magnetism and Matter Practice Question Bank
meta.chapter: NCERT Class 12 Physics Chapter 5 - Magnetism and Matter
meta.licenseNote: Original educational questions generated for this project. No copied coaching material or copyrighted question banks.

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Magnetic Dipole Moment?
questions.answer: M = m(2l)
questions.solution: Magnetic Dipole Moment: Strength of a bar magnet as a dipole. The correct relation is M = m(2l).
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: M = m(2l)
B = mu0 2M/(4 pi r^3)
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Axial Dipole Field?
questions.answer: B = mu0 2M/(4 pi r^3)
questions.solution: Axial Dipole Field: Field on axial line of short magnet. The correct relation is B = mu0 2M/(4 pi r^3).
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: B = mu0 2M/(4 pi r^3)
B = mu0 M/(4 pi r^3)
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Equatorial Dipole Field?
questions.answer: B = mu0 M/(4 pi r^3)
questions.solution: Equatorial Dipole Field: Field on equatorial line of short magnet. The correct relation is B = mu0 M/(4 pi r^3).
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: B = mu0 M/(4 pi r^3)
tau = M B sin theta
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Torque on Magnetic Dipole?
questions.answer: tau = M B sin theta
questions.solution: Torque on Magnetic Dipole: Dipole tends to align with field. The correct relation is tau = M B sin theta.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: tau = M B sin theta
U = -M B cos theta
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Dipole Potential Energy?
questions.answer: U = -M B cos theta
questions.solution: Dipole Potential Energy: Energy is minimum when dipole aligns with field. The correct relation is U = -M B cos theta.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: U = -M B cos theta
integral B dA = 0
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Gauss Law for Magnetism?
questions.answer: integral B dA = 0
questions.solution: Gauss Law for Magnetism: Net magnetic flux through closed surface is zero. The correct relation is integral B dA = 0.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: integral B dA = 0
tan I = BV/BH
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Dip Angle Relation?
questions.answer: tan I = BV/BH
questions.solution: Dip Angle Relation: Earth field components define dip angle. The correct relation is tan I = BV/BH.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: tan I = BV/BH
BH = B cos I
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Horizontal Component?
questions.answer: BH = B cos I
questions.solution: Horizontal Component: Horizontal component used by compass. The correct relation is BH = B cos I.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: BH = B cos I
BV = B sin I
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Vertical Component?
questions.answer: BV = B sin I
questions.solution: Vertical Component: Vertical component of Earth field. The correct relation is BV = B sin I.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: BV = B sin I
M = m_net/V
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Magnetization?
questions.answer: M = m_net/V
questions.solution: Magnetization: Magnetic moment per volume. The correct relation is M = m_net/V.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: M = m_net/V
B = mu0(H + M)
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents B H M Relation?
questions.answer: B = mu0(H + M)
questions.solution: B H M Relation: Total B comes from applied H and material M. The correct relation is B = mu0(H + M).
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: B = mu0(H + M)
M = chi H
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Susceptibility?
questions.answer: M = chi H
questions.solution: Susceptibility: Material magnetization response. The correct relation is M = chi H.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: M = chi H
mu_r = 1 + chi
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Relative Permeability?
questions.answer: mu_r = 1 + chi
questions.solution: Relative Permeability: Medium permeability relative to vacuum. The correct relation is mu_r = 1 + chi.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: mu_r = 1 + chi
chi = C/T
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Curie Law?
questions.answer: chi = C/T
questions.solution: Curie Law: Paramagnetic susceptibility decreases with temperature. The correct relation is chi = C/T.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: chi = C/T
loss per volume per cycle = loop area
Only the average value is required
It is independent of all variables

questions.question: Which option correctly represents Hysteresis Loss?
questions.answer: loss per volume per cycle = loop area
questions.solution: Hysteresis Loss: Energy loss in cyclic magnetization. The correct relation is loss per volume per cycle = loop area.
questions.shortcut: Draw the model first, then apply the formula.
options: loss per volume per cycle = loop area
M = m(2l)
Only the average value is required
